title: Form 13.1 — Financial Statement (Property and Support Claims)
subtitle: Ontario Family Court — Family Law Rules (FLR 13.1)
helpIntro: Form 13.1 is a detailed financial statement required when your case involves property claims (like dividing the family home or other assets), equalization of net family property, or both support AND property. It is more detailed than Form 13 because it includes a full list of your assets, debts, and a calculation of your net family property. Both parties must file this form.

fields.label: Courthouse
options: St. Catharines — Superior Court of Justice
Thunder Bay — Superior Court of Justice

fields.label: Date of this financial statement
fields.helpText: Use today's date or the date your situation is accurate as of.

fields.label: Date of marriage
fields.helpText: Required for equalization calculations.

fields.label: Date of separation
fields.helpText: The valuation date for net family property is usually the date of separation.

parts.title: Your employment and income
parts.subtitle: Step 2 of 9
parts.intro: Describe your current employment situation and sources of income.

fields.label: What is your current employment status?
Your occupation or job title
fields.placeholder: e.g. Registered Nurse, Truck Driver, Project Manager

fields.label: Annual employment income (gross, before taxes)
fields.helpText: Enter your yearly gross income from employment. Found on your T4 or pay stub.

fields.label: Annual self-employment income (net after business expenses)
Annual government benefits (EI, CPP, OAS, ODSP, Ontario Works, etc.)
Other income sources (describe and amount)
fields.placeholder:
e.g. Child tax benefit: $3,600/yr
Spouse's support payments received: $12,000/yr

fields.label: Total annual income from ALL sources
fields.helpText: Add up all the income amounts above.

parts.title: Monthly expenses
parts.subtitle: Step 3 of 9
parts.intro: List your monthly living expenses. Include only what you actually pay — not expenses shared with the other party.

fields.label: Medical and dental expenses not covered by insurance
Debt payments (credit cards, loans — minimum payments)
Other monthly expenses (describe)
fields.placeholder:
e.g. Life insurance: $80/mo
Gym membership: $45/mo

fields.label: Total monthly expenses
fields.helpText: Add up all monthly expenses above.

parts.title: Assets on the date of separation (valuation date)
parts.subtitle: Step 4 of 9
parts.intro: List every asset you owned on the date of separation. These values are used to calculate your net family property (NFP). Be as accurate as possible — you may need appraisals for real estate and businesses.

fields.label: Real estate owned on separation date (address and estimated value)
fields.placeholder:
123 Main Street, Toronto ON — Family home — Value: $850,000
456 Oak Avenue, Barrie ON — Rental property — Value: $420,000
fields.helpText: Include the matrimonial home and any other real estate. Use the fair market value on the date of separation.

fields.label: Bank accounts on separation date (bank, account type, balance)
fields.placeholder:
TD Bank — Chequing — $4,200
RBC — Savings — $12,500
TD Bank — Joint savings — $8,000 (my 50% share: $4,000)

fields.label: Investments on separation date (RRSPs, TFSAs, stocks, GICs, etc.)
fields.placeholder:
RBC RRSP — $45,000
TD TFSA — $22,000
Fidelity stock portfolio — $15,000

fields.label: Pension value on separation date
fields.helpText: Get the commuted value from your pension administrator as of the separation date.

fields.label: Vehicles on separation date (make, year, estimated value)
fields.placeholder:
2019 Toyota Camry — $18,000
2021 Honda Civic — $22,500

fields.label: Business interests on separation date (name, your share, estimated value)
fields.placeholder: Lance Contracting Ltd. — 100% owner — Value: $75,000
fields.helpText: Businesses should be professionally valuated. Use a reasonable estimate if a valuation has not been done.

fields.label: Other assets on separation date (jewellery, art, furniture, life insurance cash value, etc.)
fields.placeholder:
Life insurance (cash surrender value): $8,500
Boat and trailer: $12,000
Furniture and household goods: $5,000

fields.label: TOTAL value of all assets on date of separation
fields.helpText: Add up all the asset values listed above.

parts.title: Debts on the date of separation
parts.subtitle: Step 5 of 9
parts.intro: List every debt you owed on the date of separation. Debts are subtracted from your assets to calculate your net family property.

fields.label: Mortgages on separation date (property, lender, balance owing)
fields.placeholder:
123 Main Street — TD Bank mortgage — Balance: $420,000
456 Oak Avenue — RBC mortgage — Balance: $210,000

fields.label: Car loans on separation date
fields.placeholder: 2021 Honda Civic — TD Auto Finance — Balance: $14,500

fields.label: Credit card balances on separation date
fields.placeholder:
TD Visa — $3,200
RBC Mastercard — $1,800

fields.label: Other debts on separation date (lines of credit, personal loans, taxes owing, etc.)
fields.placeholder:
HELOC — RBC — Balance: $25,000
Personal loan — BMO — Balance: $8,000

fields.label: TOTAL debts on date of separation
fields.helpText: Add up all debt balances listed above.

parts.title: Property owned on date of marriage
parts.subtitle: Step 6 of 9
parts.intro: List assets and debts you had on the date of marriage. This amount is excluded from your net family property calculation (it was yours before the marriage).

fields.label: Assets you owned on the date of marriage (describe and value)
fields.placeholder:
RBC savings account — $8,000
2015 Dodge Ram — $25,000
RRSP balance — $12,000
fields.helpText: These amounts will be deducted from your NFP calculation. Include only assets you personally owned on the wedding day.

fields.label: Debts you owed on the date of marriage
fields.placeholder:
Student loan — $22,000
Visa credit card — $1,500
fields.helpText: Debts at marriage are also excluded — they reduce your deduction from assets at marriage.

fields.label: Net value of property owned on date of marriage (assets minus debts)
fields.helpText: Assets at marriage minus debts at marriage = this number. If negative, enter 0.

parts.title: Excluded property
parts.subtitle: Step 7 of 9
parts.intro: Certain property received during the marriage is excluded from net family property under the Family Law Act. List any excluded property you have.

fields.label: Do you have any excluded property?
fields.helpText: Excluded property includes: gifts or inheritances received during marriage, damages from a personal injury lawsuit, life insurance proceeds, and property traced to any of the above.

fields.label: Inheritances received during marriage (amount and description)
fields.placeholder:
Received from estate of John Smith (father) in 2019 — $45,000 cash
Inherited cottage in Muskoka — Value on date received: $180,000

fields.label: Gifts received from third parties during marriage (not from spouse)
fields.placeholder: Gift from parents — $25,000 used as down payment — 2018

fields.label: Damages for personal injuries received during marriage
fields.helpText: General damages for pain and suffering only — not for lost income.

parts.title: Net Family Property calculation
parts.subtitle: Step 8 of 9
parts.intro: Net Family Property (NFP) is what the court uses to determine equalization. It is calculated as: Assets at separation − Debts at separation − Property owned at marriage − Excluded property = NFP. The spouse with the higher NFP pays the other half the difference.

fields.label: Total assets on date of separation (from Step 4)
Total debts on date of separation (from Step 5)
Net property owned on date of marriage (from Step 6)
Matrimonial home exclusion deduction (enter 0 if home is being claimed as part of equalization)
fields.helpText: The matrimonial home cannot be excluded from NFP even if owned before marriage — enter 0 unless a special exception applies.

fields.label: YOUR Net Family Property total
fields.helpText: Formula: Assets at Separation − Debts at Separation − Property at Marriage − Excluded Property = NFP. If the result is negative, enter 0.

fields.label: Equalization payment you are claiming (if any)
fields.helpText: If your NFP is lower than the other party's, you may claim half the difference. Leave blank if you are the party with the higher NFP.

parts.title: Current assets and debts (today)
parts.subtitle: Step 9 of 9
parts.intro: In addition to the valuation-date figures, the court also needs your current financial picture. This section covers where things stand today.

fields.label: Real estate you own today (address and current estimated value)
fields.placeholder: 123 Main Street, Toronto ON — Current value: $920,000

fields.label: Bank accounts today (bank, type, balance)
fields.placeholder:
TD Bank — Chequing — $2,100
RBC — Savings — $9,800

fields.label: Investments today (RRSPs, TFSAs, stocks, etc.)
fields.placeholder:
RBC RRSP — $52,000
TD TFSA — $24,500

fields.label: Debts you owe today (type, lender, balance)
fields.placeholder:
TD Bank mortgage — $398,000
RBC Mastercard — $2,100

fields.label: I swear or affirm that the information in this financial statement is accurate and complete to the best of my knowledge.
fields.helpText: This form must be sworn or affirmed before a commissioner of oaths. Providing false information is contempt of court.
